statement: Einkorn wheat, the oldest cultivated wheat, is a diploid with only 14 chromosomes.
wowFactor: The original wheat was simpler than you think — just 14 chromosomes!
explanation: Einkorn (Triticum monococcum) was one of the first plants domesticated by humans around 10,000 BCE. As a diploid, it carries two sets of 7 chromosomes (2n=14). This makes it genetically simpler than the wheat on your dinner table today.
gaiaComment: 14 chromosomes and it fed entire civilizations — sometimes simpler really is mightier!
quizQuestion: How many chromosomes does einkorn wheat have?

statement: Emmer wheat is a tetraploid, meaning it has four sets of chromosomes (28 total) from two ancestral grass species fusing.
wowFactor: Emmer wheat is a hybrid of two wild grasses — nature's original GMO!
explanation: Emmer (Triticum dicoccum) arose through natural hybridization of two wild grasses, einkorn and Aegilops speltoides, followed by chromosome doubling. This polyploidization event happened naturally and gave emmer 28 chromosomes. Such hybridization events are common in plant evolution and often produce more vigorous offspring.
gaiaComment: Two grass species collided millions of years ago and accidentally invented bread wheat — nature had plans.
quizQuestion: Emmer wheat is described as a tetraploid. What does this mean?
correctAnswer: It has four sets of chromosomes
distractors: It produces four seeds per stalk
It needs four years to mature

statement: Wheat was first domesticated in the Fertile Crescent around 10,000 BCE, making it one of the oldest cultivated crops on Earth.
wowFactor: Wheat farming is 12,000 years old — older than the pyramids by 7,000 years!
explanation: The Fertile Crescent, a region stretching from modern-day Iraq through Syria, Lebanon, Israel, and into Egypt, was the birthplace of wheat agriculture. Early farmers selectively harvested wild einkorn and emmer, gradually selecting for plants with larger seeds and stalks that didn't shatter and scatter seeds before harvest.
gaiaComment: Twelve thousand years of bread-baking history — no wonder wheat feels so ancient to me.
quizQuestion: In which region was wheat first domesticated around 10,000 BCE?

statement: Gluten proteins in wheat evolved to store nitrogen in seeds — their dough-forming elasticity in baking is an accidental bonus.
wowFactor: Gluten wasn't invented for pizza dough — it's just a protein storage trick wheat repurposed!
explanation: Gluten is made of gliadin and glutenin proteins, which wheat seeds use as nitrogen reserves for the germinating seedling. When flour is mixed with water, these proteins form elastic networks that trap carbon dioxide from yeast, making dough rise. Humans exploited this chemical property thousands of years ago, long before understanding the science.
gaiaComment: Wheat was just trying to feed its babies, and humans turned the whole process into croissants.
quizQuestion: What is the original biological purpose of gluten proteins in wheat?
correctAnswer: Storing nitrogen for the germinating seedling

statement: Wheat directly feeds around 35% of the world's human population, more than any other single crop.
wowFactor: One in three humans alive today depends on wheat to survive!
explanation: Wheat is the most widely grown cereal crop and a staple food across Europe, North America, the Middle East, and Central Asia. It provides about 20% of global calories and protein. Unlike rice, which requires flooded paddies, wheat grows in a wide range of climates and altitudes, making it the most adaptable major food crop.
gaiaComment: One species of grass, and it quietly feeds a third of all humans — wheat deserves more credit.
quizQuestion: Approximately what percentage of the world's population does wheat directly feed?

statement: The earliest known bread was found at Shubayqa in Jordan and dates to 14,400 years ago — over 4,000 years before farming began.
wowFactor: Bread predates farming by 4,000 years — hunter-gatherers were baking flatbreads!
explanation: Charred flatbread fragments found at a Natufian hunter-gatherer site in Jordan push the origin of bread back to 14,400 BCE. The Natufian people ground wild cereal grains and cooked them before anyone had thought to plant crops systematically. This discovery overturns the assumption that bread-making was invented by farmers.
gaiaComment: Hunter-gatherers were baking flatbreads before anyone planted a single seed — I love a good plot twist.
quizQuestion: Where was the oldest known bread (14,400 years old) discovered?

statement: Wheat rust fungus (Puccinia) devastated Roman grain harvests so regularly that Romans held an annual festival called the Robigalia to appease the rust god.
wowFactor: Ancient Romans had a god of wheat rust — because the fungus was THAT destructive!
explanation: Wheat stem rust (Puccinia graminis) is a fungal disease that turns wheat stalks orange and kills crops. It was so devastating in ancient Rome that the Romans invented the deity Robigus (or Robigo) specifically to protect their harvests. The festival Robigalia, held on April 25th, involved sacrifices of red animals to prevent the orange rust from attacking crops.
gaiaComment: They invented a whole god just to beg a fungus to stop killing their crops — Puccinia really made an impression.
quizQuestion: What Roman festival was held to appease the god of wheat rust disease?

statement: It took approximately 10,000 years of selective breeding to transform wild einkorn into the high-yield wheat varieties we grow today.
wowFactor: Modern wheat is the result of 400 human generations of careful plant breeding!
explanation: From wild einkorn domesticated around 10,000 BCE, farmers over thousands of years selected traits like larger seeds, non-shattering stalks (so seeds stay on the plant for harvest), disease resistance, and higher yield. The Green Revolution of the 1960s then used scientific breeding to create semi-dwarf varieties with yields 5–10 times higher than traditional wheat.
gaiaComment: Ten thousand years of patient farming to build a crop that feeds billions — that's humanity's longest science experiment.
quizQuestion: Roughly how long did it take for selective breeding to transform wild einkorn into modern high-yield wheat?

statement: Modern bread wheat (Triticum aestivum) is a hexaploid with 42 chromosomes — the result of three separate grass species merging over thousands of years.
wowFactor: Modern wheat is a triple hybrid of three ancient grasses — it has triple the ancestors!
explanation: Bread wheat (T. aestivum) has 6 sets of chromosomes (hexaploid, 2n=42), combining genomes from three wild grasses: Triticum urartu (A genome), an Aegilops species (B genome), and Aegilops tauschii (D genome). The D genome was added only about 8,000 years ago in a second hybridization event, creating the highly adaptable wheat that today grows on every continent except Antarctica.
gaiaComment: Three wild grasses merged their entire genomes and accidentally created the world's most important crop.
quizQuestion: How many chromosomes does modern bread wheat (Triticum aestivum) have?

statement: Before mechanical threshers, ancient farmers used wooden flails to beat wheat stalks and separate the grain from the chaff.
wowFactor: For 10,000 years, the only way to harvest wheat was by beating it with sticks!
explanation: Threshing — separating grain from the stalks and husks — was backbreaking hand labor for most of human history. Farmers used flails (two sticks connected by a leather strap) to beat harvested wheat on a hard surface. The grain fell to the floor, while the lighter chaff could be blown away by wind (winnowing). A single person could thresh about 7 kg of grain per hour with a flail.
gaiaComment: Every loaf of bread for 10,000 years started with someone beating stalks with a stick — bread really did cost something.
quizQuestion: What tool did ancient farmers use to separate wheat grain from its stalks before mechanical threshers?
